summary: The regression was not in benchmark generation itself but in the wrapper-layer publication path: repeated semantic-artifact slices could overwrite each other because the frozen bundle filename ignored workflow assistance.
ergonomics: The benchmark runner already had a clean freeze/run split, so the fix stayed local to the two shell wrappers plus one end-to-end shell regression.
`benchmarks/test-series-summary.sh` is a useful seam because it exercises the real wrappers with a stubbed `nix` command and inspects actual frozen manifests.
Publication details are spread across wrapper scripts, runner behavior, and README examples, so small naming changes can drift unless all three are updated together.
follow_ups: Add workflow assistance as explicit benchmark metadata in `run-benchmark.mjs` outputs so summaries and packaged artifacts can report it without relying on filenames.
Factor manifest-path construction into one shared helper to prevent Codex and Claude wrapper drift on future benchmark dimensions.
If later tasks add more assistance axes, thread them through bundle/result schemas instead of only filename conventions.
warnings: Do not treat benchmark `mode` as the only publication namespace; different workflow-assistance slices can share the same mode.
The collision only appears when multiple frozen slices reuse the same note prefix, harness, model, and mode, so one-off wrapper checks can miss it.
README examples for frozen bundles must match wrapper-generated filenames or the publication guidance becomes misleading.
residual_risks: Workflow assistance is still encoded in the wrapper-generated manifest filename rather than as a first-class field inside the frozen bundle JSON or packaged publication metadata.
If callers omit `CLASP_BENCHMARK_WORKFLOW_ASSISTANCE`, wrappers fall back to `unspecified`, so distinct note prefixes are still needed to avoid collisions across otherwise identical runs.
